title: "Ebony Twilight Problem v19.71"

  description: "At 21:45, the Ebony Twilight artifact vanished from a secured alcove. Four individuals (1, 2, 3, 4) had valid clearance near that time. Exactly one suspect lies in every one of their statements; the other three are fully truthful. There is only one real culprit. Each suspect issues three statements:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'If 3 is lying, then 2 must be guilty.'
      (2) 'I left at 21:40, so I am not the thief.'
      (3) 'Suspect 4 was still near the artifact at 21:55.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) '1 did not leave before 21:50.'
      (2) 'If 4 is telling the truth, then 3 is definitely not guilty.'
      (3) 'I was not near the Ebony Twilight after 21:30.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'Either 2 is lying or 4 is guilty — but not both.'
      (2) 'I never approached the artifact after 21:35.'
      (3) '1's claim of leaving at 21:40 is surely false.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) '3 was definitely at the alcove around 21:50.'
      (2) 'I am not guilty; 2’s timeline is correct.'
      (3) 'If 1 is lying, then 3 is guilty.'

  question: "Which suspect (1, 2, 3, or 4) stole the Ebony Twilight? Provide only one digit."

answer: >
  explanation: "Predicate Logic:

  Let L(x) = 'x lies in all statements,' T(x) = 'x is fully truthful,' G(x) = 'x is guilty.'
  Exactly one L(x) is true, exactly one G(x) is true, the others T(x).

  Case L(1):
    Then 1(1),(2),(3) false => 'If 3 lying => 2 guilty' false => no forced scenario. 'Left 21:40 => not thief' is false => 1 might be thief or left later. '4 near artifact 21:55' is false => maybe 4 was not near. Meanwhile 2,3,4 are truthful => 2(1) '1 left after 21:50' must be correct, but 4(2) 'I not guilty, 2’s timeline is correct' also stands. Checking guilt quickly yields contradictions about 3’s location vs 4(1). Fails.

  Case L(2):
    Then 2(1),(2),(3) are all false => (1) '1 didn’t leave before 21:50' false => so 1's 21:40 exit stands. (2) false => if 4 is truthful => 3 is definitely not guilty is not necessarily correct => might be 3 is guilty. (3) false => 2 was near artifact after 21:30. Meanwhile 1,3,4 are truthful => 3(3) says '1's claim of 21:40 is false' but 3 is truthful => contradiction. So L(2) fails.

  Case L(3):
    Then 3(1),(2),(3) false => (1) 'Either 2 lying or 4 guilty not both' is false => might be both or neither. (2) false => 3 did approach after 21:35 => potential guilt. (3) false => 1's 21:40 claim is actually true. Then 1,2,4 are truthful => 1(2) 'Left 21:40 => I'm not thief' => G(1)=false, 2(3) 'I was not near Ebony after 21:30' => G(2)=false, 4(2) 'I am not guilty => 2's timeline is correct' => G(4)=false => that leaves G(3)=true. No contradictions.

  Case L(4):
    4(1),(2),(3) false => (1) '3 at alcove 21:50' false => maybe 3 not there, etc. 1,2,3 are truthful => time claims or who is culprit triggers mismatches with 3(2) or 1(3). Contradiction arises.

  Only L(3) scenario is consistent => G(3)=true. Ebony Twilight was stolen by suspect 3."

  final: "3"
